paper_title: Multi4D: High-Fidelity Dynamic Gaussian Splatting via Multi-Level Competitive Allocation

ranked_directions.title: Post-Training Deformation Distillation & Gaussian Quantization
ranked_directions.seed_confidence: high (explicitly mentioned by authors as limitation)
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D achieves structural compactness (25× fewer primitives) but does not apply explicit attribute compression. The 214.7 MB model could be significantly smaller.
research_questions: Can we distill the HexPlane deformation field into a lightweight MLP or lookup table without quality loss?
What is the optimal quantization strategy for the three different subset types (static, persistent, transient) given their different roles?
Can we achieve sub-50 MB model size while maintaining SOTA rendering quality?

minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Apply 8-bit quantization to SH coefficients and Gaussian covariance parameters; apply weight clustering to HexPlane deformation field
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: 30-50% storage reduction with <0.1 dB PSNR drop; identify which subset tolerates the most aggressive compression

key_papers: MEGA [53]: Memory-efficient 4DGS via temporal sensitivity analysis

ranked_directions.title: Feed-Forward / One-Shot Dynamic Scene Reconstruction
ranked_directions.seed_confidence: medium (suggested in Appendix 0.F.3 as future direction)
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D and all current dynamic GS methods require per-scene optimization (20k iterations, ~1.2h). Feed-forward approaches could enable real-time reconstruction from monocular video.
research_questions: Can we train a feed-forward network that predicts the three-level decomposition (ℊₛ, ℊ𝒹, ℊₜ) directly from video input?
How should the inductive biases (inverse expressiveness initialization) be encoded in a feed-forward architecture?
Can competitive allocation emerge from a single forward pass rather than iterative optimization?

minimal_validation_experiment.dataset: PointOdyssey [54] synthetic dataset or Aria Digital Twin [30]
minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Small transformer-based predictor that takes video frame features + depth and outputs three Gaussian sets; fine-tune with ℒ_total from Multi4D
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: 50-80% of per-scene optimization quality with <100 ms inference; identify the hardest scenes for feed-forward prediction
minimal_validation_experiment.compute_budget: 4-8 GPU-days on RTX 4090 (pretraining on synthetic data)

key_papers: Feed-forward bullet-time [23]: Monocular video → dynamic reconstruction

ranked_directions.title: Temporally-Consistent Dynamic Scene Editing Using Decomposed Representation
ranked_directions.seed_confidence: medium (natural extension of Multi4D's structured decomposition)
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D's explicit separation into static, persistent-dynamic, and transient components enables direct editing of each layer independently while preserving temporal consistency.
research_questions: Can we edit the static background without affecting moving objects by modifying only ℊₛ?
Can we replace or remove moving objects by operating on ℊ𝒹 while ℊₜ automatically adapts?
Can we alter appearance (e.g., relighting) by constraining ℊₜ while preserving ℊ𝒹 geometry?

minimal_validation_experiment.dataset: Neu3D (Coffee Martini — complex background + moving object)
minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Freeze ℊ𝒹 and ℊₜ, re-optimize ℊₛ with edited background texture; vice versa edit ℊ𝒹 to remove/relocate objects
minimal_validation_experiment.baseline: TRASE [21] editing or ground-truth masked composition
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Plausible edited renderings with temporal consistency; quantify editing fidelity via user study or FID
minimal_validation_experiment.compute_budget: 1-2 GPU-hours on RTX 4090 per editing operation

key_papers: SC-GS [9]: Sparse-controlled GS for editable dynamic scenes

ranked_directions.title: Multi-Scale Transient Modeling with Hierarchical Temporal Granularity
ranked_directions.seed_confidence: medium (addressing why ℊₜ has only one level)
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D uses a single transient level ℊₜ. But dynamic scenes have phenomena at multiple temporal scales (e.g., smoke vs. cloth fluttering vs. specular flash). Hierarchical transient levels could improve efficiency.
research_questions: Should transient modeling be further decomposed into multiple temporal-frequency bands (low-freq transient vs. high-freq transient)?
Can we apply different 4D covariance regularization based on the expected temporal frequency of each transient primitive?
Would multiple transient levels improve generalization to scenes with both slow and fast transient effects?

minimal_validation_experiment.dataset: Technicolor (Theater — has diverse temporal phenomena) + NeRF-DS (all scenes)
minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Add a second transient level ℊₜ₂ with larger temporal variance (more persistent, fewer primitives); modify ℒ_diversity to also penalize overlap between ℊₜ₁ and ℊₜ₂
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Similar or better PSNR with 10-20% further reduction in total transient primitives; better separation of e.g., fire (long-lived) vs. specular glints (very short-lived)

key_papers: Motion Layering [5]: 4D Gaussian videos with motion layering

ranked_directions.title: End-to-End Open-Vocabulary 4D Segmentation with Multi4D Features
ranked_directions.seed_confidence: medium (current pipeline has two-stage: reconstruction → semantic)
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D provides compact persistent Gaussians (13k dynamic) ideal for semantic embedding. Current pipeline reconstructs first, then adds semantics. Joint optimization could improve both tasks.
research_questions: Can semantic feature learning be integrated into the reconstruction phase to jointly optimize geometry and semantics?
Would joint optimization improve tracking accuracy through semantic-aware deformation fields?
Can CLIP/DINO features be directly distilled into ℊ𝒹 Gaussians during training?

minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Add semantic feature head to ℊ𝒹 during Phase II of Multi4D training; apply contrastive loss alongside ℒ_color
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: 0.920+ mIoU (+0.006 over two-stage); potentially cleaner segmentation boundaries; slightly slower training but faster inference

ranked_directions.title: All-Point Tracking via Persistent Gaussian Correspondence
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D's persistent Gaussians ℊ𝒹 maintain temporal identity through the deformation field, providing a natural substrate for long-term point tracking without per-frame correspondence.
research_questions: How do tracking accuracy metrics (e.g., from TAP-Vid benchmark) compare between Multi4D Gaussian tracks and dedicated tracking methods (CoTracker)?
Can ℊ𝒹 centroid trajectories directly serve as point tracks with sub-pixel accuracy?
What is the minimum number of persistent Gaussians needed for reliable tracking?

minimal_validation_experiment.dataset: PointOdyssey [54] synthetic benchmark with ground-truth point trajectories
minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Extract ℊ𝒹 centroid trajectories from Multi4D reconstruction; compare against ground-truth point tracks using standard tracking metrics (trajectory accuracy, occlusion accuracy)
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Competitive tracking accuracy in well-textured regions; worse in textureless regions where persistent Gaussians are sparse; identify failure modes
minimal_validation_experiment.compute_budget: 2 GPU-hours on RTX 4090 (inference only, Multi4D already trained)

key_papers: CoTracker [12]: Joint tracking of points in video

ranked_directions.title: Multi4D for Egocentric / Surgical Video Reconstruction
ranked_directions.seed_confidence: low (domain adaptation; Multi4D authors have prior work in surgical domain [38])
ranked_directions.motivation: Multi4D was evaluated on multi-view lab settings and monocular specular scenes. Egocentric video (e.g., surgical procedures, AR/VR) presents unique challenges: rapid camera motion, self-occlusion, deformation of non-rigid tissues.
research_questions: Can Multi4D handle the extreme camera motion typical of egocentric videos?
How does the competitive allocation perform for surgical scenes where both tool (rigid motion) and tissue (non-rigid deformation) occur?
Can ℊₜ model surgical smoke, blood splatter, and instrument reflections?

minimal_validation_experiment.dataset: POV-Surgery [38] (egocentric surgical video dataset from same lab group)
minimal_validation_experiment.architecture: Apply Multi4D training pipeline directly to POV-Surgery sequences; evaluate rendering quality + dynamic mask quality
minimal_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Multi4D should outperform baselines for specular surgical scenes; key failure mode: very rapid camera motion may break COLMAP initialization for ℊₛ

key_papers: POV-Surgery [38]: Egocentric hand and tool pose estimation during surgical activities
EgoGaussian [52]: Dynamic scene understanding from egocentric video

criteria: Feasibility (can be validated with small compute within existing Multi4D codebase)
Impact (addresses a clear limitation or opens a significant new capability)
Immediacy (leverages existing Multi4D outputs without major architectural changes)
Novelty (not already covered by concurrent work)
ranking_rationale.notes: Rank 1 (compression) is the most immediately actionable since the authors explicitly identify it. Rank 2 (feed-forward) is higher risk but highest long-term impact. Ranks 3-5 leverage Multi4D's core strength (structured decomposition) for new capabilities. Ranks 6-7 are speculative extensions to new applications.
